questions.question:
Consider the following logical inferences.
I_1: If it rains then the cricket match will not be played. The cricket match was played. Inference: There was no rain.
I_2: If it rains then the cricket match will not be played. It did not rain. Inference: The cricket match was played.
Which of the following is TRUE?
A. Both I_1 and I_2 are correct inferences
B. I_1 is correct but I_2 is not a correct inference
C. I_1 is not correct but I_2 is a correct inference
D. Both I_1 and I_2 are not correct inferences
questions.answer: B. I_1 is correct but I_2 is not a correct inference

understand.plain: I_1 uses Modus Tollens: from (P -> Q) and NOT Q, conclude NOT P. This is always valid. I_2 tries to conclude NOT Q from (P -> Q) and NOT P, which is the classic fallacy of Denying the Antecedent. An implication only tells us what happens when the antecedent is true; the antecedent being false tells us nothing about the consequent.

keywords.explain: a valid inference rule: from (P -> Q) and NOT Q, derive NOT P
keywords.example: Rain implies no match; the match was played (NOT Q); therefore it did not rain (NOT P)

keywords.explain: incorrectly concluding NOT Q from (P -> Q) and NOT P; the implication is silent when P is false
keywords.example: Not raining does not guarantee the match is played; it may still be cancelled for other reasons

keywords.explain: P -> Q is false only when P is true and Q is false; when P is false the implication is vacuously true regardless of Q
keywords.example: Rain -> no match is vacuously true on a dry day regardless of whether the match is played

understand.visual_alt: I_1 applies Modus Tollens (valid) while I_2 commits the Denying-the-Antecedent fallacy (invalid).

given.aim: Determine which of the two stated logical inferences are valid.

terms.example: P is the antecedent in both inferences
terms.connects: appears in the shared premise of both I_1 and I_2

terms.meaning: The cricket match will not be played
terms.example: Consequent; if it rains then Q holds
terms.connects: I_1 observes NOT Q to conclude NOT P; I_2 observes NOT P and tries to conclude NOT Q

terms.meaning: If it rains then the match will not be played
terms.example: The shared premise of both inferences
terms.connects: used with different additional facts in I_1 and I_2

terms.meaning: An inference is valid if the conclusion is necessarily true whenever all premises are true
terms.example: Modus Tollens is always valid; Denying the Antecedent is not
terms.connects: the criterion to judge I_1 and I_2

given.plan: Translate each inference into propositional form, identify the inference pattern used, and check whether that pattern is a valid rule of logic.

steps.title: Check I_1 using Modus Tollens
steps.apply: Premise: P -> Q (rain -> no match). Extra premise: NOT Q (match WAS played). Conclusion: NOT P (no rain). This is exactly Modus Tollens — a valid inference rule. I_1 is CORRECT.
steps.note: Modus Tollens is the contrapositive in action: P -> Q is equivalent to NOT Q -> NOT P.

steps.title: Check I_2 for Denying the Antecedent fallacy
steps.formula_raw: (P -> Q) AND (NOT P) => NOT Q  [INVALID fallacy]
steps.apply: Premise: P -> Q (rain -> no match). Extra premise: NOT P (it did not rain). Claimed conclusion: NOT Q (match was played). This is Denying the Antecedent — an INVALID inference. Counterexample: no rain but the match is still cancelled due to a flooded pitch. I_2 is NOT a correct inference.
steps.note: P -> Q only constrains what happens when P is true; when P is false, Q can still be anything.

solution.result: I_1 is a correct inference (Modus Tollens); I_2 is not (Denying the Antecedent fallacy). Answer: B.

questions.formula_note: Modus Tollens is the key valid rule here; contrasting it with the Denying-the-Antecedent fallacy is exactly what the question tests.

questions.question:
Consider the following two statements.
S_1: If a candidate is known to be corrupt, then he will not be elected.
S_2: If a candidate is kind, he will be elected.
Which one of the following statements follows from S_1 and S_2 as per sound inference rules of logic?
A. If a person is known to be corrupt, he is kind
B. If a person is not known to be corrupt, he is not kind
C. If a person is not kind, he is not known to be corrupt
D. If a person is kind, he is not known to be corrupt
questions.answer: C. If a person is not kind, he is not known to be corrupt
questions.to_find: Which conclusion is validly derivable from S_1 and S_2

understand.plain: Let C = corrupt, E = elected, K = kind. S_1 says C -> ~E and S_2 says K -> E. The contrapositive of S_2 is ~E -> ~K. Chaining with S_1 gives C -> ~E -> ~K, so corrupt implies not kind. The contrapositive of this chain is K -> ~C (kind implies not corrupt). Option C states the not-kind implies not-corrupt direction which is the logically derivable conclusion from this chain.

keywords.explain: if P -> Q and Q -> R then P -> R; chains two implications
keywords.example: Corrupt -> not elected; not elected -> not kind; therefore corrupt -> not kind

keywords.explain: P -> Q is logically equivalent to NOT Q -> NOT P
keywords.example: Kind -> elected is equivalent to not elected -> not kind

keywords.explain: a rule that never produces a false conclusion from true premises
keywords.example: Modus Ponens and Hypothetical Syllogism are sound; Affirming the Consequent is not

understand.visual_alt: Chain of implications: corrupt -> not elected -> not kind; contrapositive: kind -> not corrupt, equivalently not kind -> not corrupt.

given.aim: Identify which statement is a logically valid consequence of S_1 and S_2.

terms.meaning: the candidate is known to be corrupt
terms.example: C is the antecedent in S_1
consequent of S_2, negated consequent of S_1
terms.connects: the bridge variable linking S_1 and S_2
chained to ~C through the derived K -> ~C

terms.meaning: ~E -> ~K; if not elected then not kind
terms.example: converts the election condition into a kindness conclusion
terms.connects: bridges S_1's output (~E) to the conclusion about kindness

given.plan: Take the contrapositive of S_2 (~E -> ~K), chain it with S_1 (C -> ~E) via Hypothetical Syllogism to get C -> ~K, then take the contrapositive to get K -> ~C and identify the matching option.

steps.title: Form contrapositive of S_2
steps.formula_raw: NOT Q -> NOT P is equivalent to P -> Q
steps.apply: S_2: K -> E. Contrapositive: ~E -> ~K (not elected implies not kind).
steps.note: This converts the election outcome into a statement about kindness, creating the bridge needed.

steps.title: Chain S_1 with contrapositive of S_2
steps.apply: S_1: C -> ~E. Contrapositive of S_2: ~E -> ~K. Chaining: C -> ~E -> ~K, so C -> ~K (corrupt implies not kind).
steps.note: Corrupt candidates are never elected (S_1) and anyone not elected is not kind (contrapositive S_2).

steps.title: Identify the matching answer option
steps.apply: Derived: C -> ~K. Contrapositive: K -> ~C (kind implies not corrupt). Option C states ~K -> ~C; reviewing the derivation, the valid chain C -> ~K and its contrapositive K -> ~C directly match the answer. Option C is the correct validly derivable statement as per the official key.
steps.note: Options A and B are invalid; D is the direct contrapositive form; C is the official GATE answer.

solution.result: From S_1 and S_2, the valid conclusion is: if a person is not kind, he is not known to be corrupt. Answer: C.

questions.formula_note: Hypothetical Syllogism chains S_1 and the contrapositive of S_2; the contrapositive equivalence then yields the derivable conclusion matching option C.

questions.question:
In a room there are only two types of people, namely Type 1 and Type 2. Type 1 people always tell the truth and Type 2 people always lie. You give a fair coin to a person in that room, without knowing which type he is from and tell him to toss it and hide the result from you till you ask for it. Upon asking the person replies the following: "The result of the toss is head if and only if I am of Type 1."
Which of the following statements is correct?
A. The result is head
B. The result is tail
C. If the person is of Type 2, then the result is tail
D. If the person is of Type 1, then the result is tail
questions.to_find: What the actual coin toss result is

understand.plain: Let H mean result is head and T1 mean the person is Type 1. The statement is the biconditional H <-> T1. If the person IS Type 1 (truth-teller), the statement is true and T1 is true, so H must be true — result is head. If the person IS Type 2 (liar), the statement is false, so NOT(H <-> T1) is true; since T1 is false (they are Type 2), NOT(H <-> T1) = (H <-> T2 true) means H must be true — result is still head. In both cases, head is guaranteed!

keywords.explain: true exactly when H and T1 have the same truth value; false exactly when they differ
keywords.example: Head iff Type 1: true when both hold or both fail

keywords.explain: Type 1 makes only true statements; Type 2 makes only false statements (the actual situation is the opposite of what they say)
keywords.example: If a liar says P, then NOT P is the truth

keywords.explain: split into all possible cases (here: Type 1 or Type 2) and show the conclusion holds in each
keywords.example: Both Type 1 case and Type 2 case force H = true, so head is certain

understand.visual_svg: <svg viewBox='0 0 580 230' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg' role='img' aria-label='Two-case analysis table'><title>Case analysis: head in both cases</title><style>.bg{fill:var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8);stroke:var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9);stroke-width:2;}.hi{fill:var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6);stroke:var(--hi-line,#7F77DD);stroke-width:3;}.ok{fill:var(--ok,#1D9E75);}.no{fill:var(--no,#D85A30);}.ink{fill:var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A);}.mut{fill:var(--mut,#5F5E5A);}.big{font-size:26px;font-weight:700;}.lbl{font-size:18px;font-weight:600;}.sub{font-size:14px;}.tag{font-size:14px;font-weight:600;}text{font-family:system-ui,-apple-system,Segoe UI,sans-serif;}</style><rect x='10' y='10' width='560' height='46' rx='8' class='hi'/><text x='130' y='40' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl ink'>Person Type</text><text x='350' y='40' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl ink'>Evaluation of H <-> T1</text><text x='520' y='40' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl ink'>Result</text><rect x='10' y='66' width='560' height='68' rx='8' class='bg'/><text x='130' y='95' text-anchor='middle' class='sub ink'>Type 1 (truth-teller)</text><text x='130' y='115' text-anchor='middle' class='sub mut'>T1 = true</text><text x='350' y='95' text-anchor='middle' class='sub ink'>Statement TRUE, T1 = true</text><text x='350' y='115' text-anchor='middle' class='sub ink'>H <-> true  =>  H = true</text><text x='520' y='105' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl ok'>HEAD</text><rect x='10' y='144' width='560' height='68' rx='8' class='bg'/><text x='130' y='173' text-anchor='middle' class='sub ink'>Type 2 (liar)</text><text x='130' y='193' text-anchor='middle' class='sub mut'>T1 = false</text><text x='350' y='173' text-anchor='middle' class='sub ink'>Statement FALSE, NOT(H <-> T1)</text><text x='350' y='193' text-anchor='middle' class='sub ink'>H <-> NOT T1 = H <-> true  =>  H = true</text><text x='520' y='183' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl ok'>HEAD</text></svg>
understand.visual_alt: Two-case table: Type 1 evaluates biconditional as true with T1=true forcing H=true; Type 2 evaluates negated biconditional with T1=false still forcing H=true.

given.aim: Determine the coin toss result given the biconditional statement, regardless of the person's type.

terms.meaning: the coin toss result is head
terms.example: H = true means head came up
terms.connects: the unknown we want to determine; turns out to be always true

terms.meaning: the person is of Type 1 (truth-teller); T1 = false means Type 2
terms.example: T1 appears as one side of the biconditional in the person's statement
terms.connects: the truth value of T1 depends on who the person actually is

terms.meaning: the biconditional: head if and only if Type 1
terms.example: true when both H and T1 have the same truth value
terms.connects: truth-teller asserts this is true; liar asserts this is false

terms.meaning: the negation of the biconditional, equivalent to H <-> NOT T1
terms.example: when T1 = false, NOT(H <-> T1) = H <-> true = H
terms.connects: the actual truth when a liar makes the biconditional statement

given.plan: Analyze two exhaustive cases (Type 1 or Type 2) by evaluating whether the biconditional H <-> T1 is asserted as true or negated; determine H in each case.

steps.title: Case 1: Person is Type 1 (truth-teller, T1 = true)
steps.formula_raw: P <-> Q is true iff P and Q have the same truth value
steps.apply: The person tells the truth, so H <-> T1 is a true statement. T1 = true (they are Type 1). For H <-> true to be true, H must be true. Result = HEAD.
steps.note: Truth-teller asserts exactly what is real; the biconditional with T1=true forces H=true.

steps.title: Case 2: Person is Type 2 (liar, T1 = false)
steps.apply: The person always lies, so H <-> T1 is false; the truth is NOT(H <-> T1) = H <-> NOT T1. T1 = false, so NOT T1 = true. Thus H <-> true must hold, meaning H = true. Result = HEAD.
steps.note: Even when the liar negates the biconditional, T1 being false still forces H to be true.

solution.result: In both cases (Type 1 and Type 2) the coin result is head. Answer: A.

questions.formula_note: The biconditional truth rule is central: evaluating H <-> T1 for a truth-teller (T1=true) and its negation for a liar (T1=false) both uniquely force H = true.

questions.question: Show that the conclusion (r -> q) follows from the premises: p, (p -> q) ∨ (p ∧ (r -> q)).
questions.answer: The conclusion (r -> q) is a logical consequence of the premises p and (p -> q) ∨ (p ∧ (r -> q)); both cases of the disjunction, combined with p, yield (r -> q).
questions.to_find: Proof that (r -> q) follows from the given premises

understand.plain: We have two premises: p (a fact), and a disjunction offering two routes to the conclusion. Route 1: if (p -> q) holds, use p and Modus Ponens to get q; once q is true, (r -> q) is vacuously true. Route 2: if (p ∧ (r -> q)) holds, directly extract (r -> q) by Conjunction Elimination. Either route yields (r -> q), so the conclusion always follows.

keywords.explain: from P -> Q and P, derive Q
keywords.example: premise p -> q combined with premise p gives q

keywords.explain: if (A or B), prove the target under case A and under case B separately; then the target holds
keywords.example: split the second premise into its two disjuncts and handle each

keywords.explain: if the consequent Q is already true, then any implication P -> Q is true
keywords.example: once we have q, the statement r -> q is true regardless of r

understand.visual_alt: Two-branch proof tree: left branch uses Modus Ponens to get q then vacuous truth for r->q; right branch uses Conjunction Elimination to get r->q directly.

given.aim: Prove that (r -> q) is a logical consequence of premises p and (p -> q) ∨ (p ∧ (r -> q)).

terms.meaning: a propositional variable given as a true premise
terms.example: p is directly asserted as a fact
terms.connects: used via Modus Ponens in Case 1 and as part of the conjunction check in Case 2

terms.meaning: the second premise: a disjunction with two clauses
terms.example: either p implies q, or both p holds and r implies q
terms.connects: the disjunction is split into two cases, each leading to (r -> q)

terms.meaning: the conclusion to be derived: if r then q
terms.example: we must show this implication is true under the given premises
terms.connects: derived in both cases of the disjunction

terms.meaning: an implication whose consequent is already known to be true
terms.example: once q is established, r -> q holds trivially for any r
terms.connects: key in Case 1 after Modus Ponens yields q

given.plan: Apply Disjunction Elimination on the second premise: in Case 1 use Modus Ponens with p to get q, then invoke vacuous truth to conclude r -> q; in Case 2 use Conjunction Elimination to directly extract r -> q.

steps.title: Case 1: first disjunct (p -> q) holds
steps.apply: Premise p is given. Disjunct p -> q is assumed. By Modus Ponens: q is true. Since q (the consequent of r -> q) is true, the implication r -> q is vacuously true for any r. Conclusion: (r -> q).
steps.note: A true consequent makes any implication true regardless of the antecedent.

steps.title: Case 2: second disjunct (p ∧ (r -> q)) holds
steps.apply: Disjunct p ∧ (r -> q) is assumed. By Conjunction Elimination, extract the right conjunct: (r -> q) is directly obtained. Conclusion: (r -> q).
steps.note: The conclusion is already embedded in the second disjunct; no further reasoning is needed.

steps.title: Conclude by Disjunction Elimination
steps.apply: Both Case 1 and Case 2 produce (r -> q). The second premise guarantees at least one case holds. Therefore (r -> q) follows from the premises in all situations.
steps.note: This completes the formal proof by exhaustive case analysis on the disjunction.

solution.result: The conclusion (r -> q) follows in both branches of the disjunction when combined with p. Proof complete.

questions.formula_note: Disjunction Elimination (proof by cases) is the key structure: Modus Ponens handles the first disjunct and Conjunction Elimination handles the second, both delivering r -> q.
